questions.question: What is the primary purpose of project time planning?
options: To estimate the project's total budget
To organize project activities into a realistic schedule
To identify project stakeholders
To approve the Business Case
questions.explanation: Time planning creates a realistic schedule that helps coordinate activities and achieve the project's objectives.

questions.question: Which project management tool is commonly used to break down project deliverables into smaller pieces of work before scheduling?
options: Stakeholder Register
Risk Register
Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)
Project Charter
questions.explanation: The WBS decomposes project deliverables into manageable work packages, which are later converted into project activities.

questions.question: Which dependency type means that one activity cannot begin until another activity has been completed?
options: Start-to-Start (SS)
Finish-to-Finish (FF)
Finish-to-Start (FS)
Start-to-Finish (SF)
questions.explanation: Finish-to-Start is the most common dependency relationship and requires one activity to finish before another can begin.

questions.question: What does an activity duration estimate represent?
options: The number of employees assigned to the task
The expected time required to complete an activity
The activity's total cost
The amount of float available
questions.explanation: Duration estimates predict how long an activity is expected to take under normal working conditions.

questions.question: Why is sequencing activities an important step in project planning?
options: It determines the logical order in which work should be performed
It reduces the project budget
It eliminates all project risks
It assigns project team members to activities
questions.explanation: Activity sequencing identifies logical relationships and establishes the order in which work should be completed.

questions.question: Why is it important to identify activity dependencies before developing the project schedule?
options: Because dependencies determine the logical flow of work and influence the overall schedule
Because dependencies reduce project costs
Because every activity should depend on another activity
Because dependencies replace duration estimates
questions.explanation: Dependencies define how activities relate to one another and ensure the schedule reflects realistic project execution.

questions.question: Why is bottom-up estimating generally considered more accurate than analogous estimating?
options: It estimates each activity individually before combining the estimates
It always produces the lowest project cost
It ignores uncertainty
It only requires historical data
questions.explanation: Bottom-up estimating uses detailed estimates for individual activities, resulting in greater overall accuracy.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of identifying the Critical Path?
options: To determine which activities require the highest budget
To identify the sequence of activities that determines the project's shortest possible duration
To identify which employees are busiest
To eliminate project risks
questions.explanation: The Critical Path determines the minimum possible project duration and highlights activities that cannot be delayed without affecting project completion.

questions.question: What does float (also called slack) represent?
options: Unused project budget
The time an activity can be delayed without affecting the project's completion date
The number of backup resources available
The difference between planned and actual cost
questions.explanation: Float represents scheduling flexibility. Activities with positive float can be delayed without delaying the overall project.

questions.question: A construction project cannot begin installing windows until the building frame has been completed. Which type of dependency does this illustrate?
options: Start-to-Start (SS)
Finish-to-Finish (FF)
Finish-to-Start (FS)
Start-to-Finish (SF)
questions.explanation: The window installation cannot start until the framing activity has finished, making it a Finish-to-Start dependency.

questions.question: A project manager notices that an activity has 4 days of float. What does this mean?
options: The activity must finish four days early
The activity can be delayed by up to four days without delaying the project's completion
The activity is on the Critical Path
The activity requires four additional workers
questions.explanation: Activities with positive float have scheduling flexibility and may be delayed without affecting the project's finish date.

questions.question: A client asks for the project to finish two weeks earlier without changing the scope. Which schedule compression technique involves adding more resources to critical activities?
options: Fast Tracking
Bottom-up Estimating
Crashing
Rolling Wave Planning
questions.explanation: Crashing shortens the schedule by assigning additional resources to activities on the Critical Path.

questions.question: A project manager decides to start electrical installation before all construction work is complete in order to save time. Which schedule compression technique is being used?
options: Critical Path Analysis
Three-point Estimating
Fast Tracking
Resource Leveling
questions.explanation: Fast Tracking overlaps activities that were originally planned sequentially, reducing project duration but increasing risk.

questions.question: During project execution, actual progress is slower than planned. What should the Project Manager do first?
options: Immediately increase the project budget
Compare actual progress with the approved schedule baseline
Remove activities from the project scope
Delay all remaining activities
questions.explanation: The first step is to compare actual performance against the approved schedule baseline to understand the extent and cause of the delay.

questions.question: Which tool is most commonly used to communicate the project schedule visually to stakeholders?
options: Risk Matrix
Business Case
Gantt Chart
Stakeholder Register
questions.explanation: A Gantt Chart displays project activities as bars along a timeline, making schedules easy to understand.

questions.question: A project has experienced several delays, but none of the delayed activities are on the Critical Path. What is the most likely impact?
options: The project will automatically finish late
The project may still finish on time if the available float is not exceeded
The project budget becomes invalid
The project must immediately be crashed
questions.explanation: Delays on non-critical activities may be absorbed by available float without affecting the project's completion date.

questions.question: Why is the Critical Path often monitored more closely than other project activities?
options: Because it always contains the most expensive activities
Because delays on the Critical Path directly delay the entire project
Because it contains every project activity
Because it has the largest number of resources
questions.explanation: Critical activities have little or no float, meaning delays directly impact the project's finish date.

questions.question: A schedule has been created without considering resource availability. What is the biggest risk?
options: The project may appear feasible on paper but be impossible to execute in practice
The schedule will automatically reduce project costs
Dependencies become unnecessary
The Critical Path can no longer be identified
questions.explanation: A schedule must account for resource constraints to be realistic and achievable.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the Schedule Baseline?
options: The project's first draft schedule
The approved schedule used to measure project performance
A list of activity dependencies
The supplier's delivery schedule
questions.explanation: The Schedule Baseline is the approved version of the schedule against which actual progress is measured.

questions.question: Senior management requests that a project be completed one month earlier. Before deciding how to achieve this, what should the Project Manager evaluate?
options: Only the preferences of the project team
The trade-offs between time, cost, scope, quality and risk
Whether the project sponsor agrees
Only the available project budget
questions.explanation: Reducing project duration usually affects other project constraints. The Project Manager should evaluate the impact on cost, scope, quality and risk before deciding on a solution.

questions.question: A project team discovers that one activity on the Critical Path will be delayed by three days. No corrective action is taken. What is the most likely outcome?
options: Only the activity budget will increase
The overall project completion date will likely be delayed by three days
The delay will automatically be absorbed by float
Only non-critical activities will be affected
questions.explanation: Activities on the Critical Path typically have zero float, so delays directly impact the project's completion date.

questions.question: A project manager is estimating activity durations for a project unlike any completed before. Which estimating technique would be most valuable?
options: Expert judgment
Ignoring uncertainty
Using last year's project budget
Assuming every activity takes one week
questions.explanation: When little historical data exists, expert judgment provides valuable knowledge and realistic estimates.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the relationship between the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) and the project schedule?
options: The schedule is created before the WBS
The WBS identifies the work that is later scheduled as project activities
The WBS replaces the project schedule
The WBS is only used after project completion
questions.explanation: The WBS defines the project work, while the schedule determines when that work will be performed.

questions.question: A project team chooses to overlap two design activities to save time. Midway through the project, one design changes, requiring the other activity to be repeated. What is the main disadvantage illustrated?
options: Fast Tracking can increase the risk of rework
Crashing always increases project costs
Critical Path analysis becomes impossible
Activity sequencing is unnecessary
questions.explanation: Fast Tracking reduces project duration but increases the likelihood of rework if earlier activities change.

questions.question: Why should activity duration estimates be reviewed as the project progresses?
options: Because schedules should never remain unchanged when new information becomes available
Because every activity should become shorter
Because the Critical Path disappears during execution
Because budgets cannot be updated
questions.explanation: Projects evolve over time, and updated information often improves the accuracy of remaining duration estimates.

questions.question: A project manager reports that the project is exactly on schedule, but several critical activities are only 20% complete despite being planned at 50%. What does this suggest?
options: The schedule reporting may be inaccurate and requires further investigation
The project will definitely finish early
Critical activities are unimportant
The project budget should be doubled
questions.explanation: If critical activities are significantly behind plan, the reported schedule status should be questioned and investigated.

questions.question: A client insists that every project activity should start as early as possible. Why might this not be the best scheduling approach?
options: It may create unnecessary resource conflicts and reduce schedule flexibility
It guarantees project delays
It removes the Critical Path
It eliminates dependencies
questions.explanation: Starting everything as early as possible can overload resources and reduce the Project Manager's flexibility to manage the schedule effectively.

questions.question: Two schedule compression options are available. Option A reduces the schedule by one week with little additional risk. Option B reduces the schedule by two weeks but significantly increases the probability of costly rework. What is the most appropriate approach?
options: Always choose the largest schedule reduction
Evaluate the trade-off between time savings, risk and project objectives before making a decision
Reject both options immediately
Always choose the cheapest option regardless of the schedule
questions.explanation: Project Managers must balance competing constraints. The fastest solution is not always the best if it introduces unacceptable risks.

questions.question: Why is project scheduling considered an ongoing management process rather than a one-time planning activity?
options: Because projects continuously change as new information, risks and progress become known
Because the original schedule is always incorrect
Because schedules expire every month
Because only stakeholders update the schedule
questions.explanation: A project schedule should be continuously monitored and updated to reflect actual progress and changing project conditions.

questions.question: What is the most important responsibility of a Project Manager when managing the project schedule?
options: To ensure the schedule remains realistic, monitor progress continuously and take corrective action when necessary
To avoid changing the schedule under any circumstances
To focus only on non-critical activities
To let stakeholders manage all schedule changes
questions.explanation: Effective schedule management requires continuous monitoring, realistic forecasting and timely corrective actions to keep the project on track.
